narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting whether someone is likely to pass on their first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10, LSAT score of 30, full-time enrollment plans, and your low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 grades—and calculated a 77% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this to prioritize admits with stronger chances of bar success. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, signaling consistently low performance relative to peers that strongly raised red flags for bar readiness. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 1, also far under the average of about 5.7, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that carried forward. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.10 was below the dataset average of 3.21 and initially concerning, it actually worked slightly in your favor compared to what the model expected for someone with your other traits, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing. Overall, your academic deciles stood out as much weaker than those of typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher and pair them with stronger test scores like the average LSAT of 36.7. Your full-time status aligns with most applicants at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much. This combination painted a picture of higher bar failure risk than the model sees in successful profiles. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above 30, as that could offset your decile rankings in future applications. Consider targeted GPA improvement programs or post-bac courses to demonstrate upward trends in your academic deciles. Many students in similar situations reapply successfully after addressing these core weaknesses—your middle-income background shows resilience, so channel that into concrete steps like tutoring or exam prep to shift your predicted odds.
